summary: Benn argues that the data tooling industry is shifting from vertically integrated, all-in-one products to a horizontally integrated “stack” where each layer (ingestion, storage, transformation, consumption) is owned by specialized tools. This change is driven by the explosion of vendors and the need to blend data across functions, but it creates new challenges around integration and boundary discipline.
money_quotes: In response to these changes, companies are turning their stacks sideways.
One tool is responsible for each layer—each stage of the data supply chain—across the entire business.
Data teams are better off not trying to cut against the grain of this emerging architectural outline, and vendors are better off not encouraging them to do so.
Horizontally integrated companies, however, can’t operate in a vacuum; for this orientation to work, these companies have to work well with the rest of the supply chain.
When they do, however, it won’t be a single product.
key_insight: The modern data stack is evolving toward horizontal specialization, which improves flexibility but makes interoperability and clear architectural boundaries the central challenges.
